summary: Gruber dismantles Kevin Roose's NYT column arguing that Apple's new per-contact sharing controls in iOS 18 are anticompetitive. He points out that the previous all-or-nothing contact access was a privacy failure that companies exploited as a growth-hacking technique, and that granular controls are long overdue. He rejects the claim that Apple's first-party apps having full contact access is unfair, arguing that deep integration is the entire point of Apple's platform and what users choose it for. He draws an analogy between companies resenting new privacy controls and polluters resenting environmental regulations, framing the complaint as privileged actors objecting to losing an exploitative advantage.
key_points: iOS 18's per-contact sharing controls are a straightforward privacy improvement, not an anticompetitive move — the real surprise is it took Apple this long
Companies that built social graphs by slurping entire address books were exploiting a creepy growth-hacking technique with real consequences for users
First-party apps having full contact access is inherent to platform integration, which is why people choose Apple products in the first place
The psychology of resenting new restrictions mirrors how polluters resent environmental regulations — the privilege was always illegitimate
The right question is whether users want and expect these controls, not whether VC-backed growth hackers find them inconvenient
money_quotes: It's rare that a story is worse than its provocative headline, but this one manages it.
If iOS had debuted in 2007 with per-contact sharing controls exactly like those in iOS 18 today, no one serious would ever have complained that this was wrong or unfair.
They're not just giving users control they previously didn't have, they're taking something away from companies that seek to exploit, as Heer aptly describes it, a creepy growth-hacking technique.

Writ large, this psychology explains why granting social equality to minority groups feels to some in the majority group, the small-minded and bigoted, like a loss of privilege and a downgrade in status.
To the first party go the first-party spoils.
People choose to use Apple platforms because of the integration, not despite it.
A new social media network is not an address book — and users know it.
The question to ask is, 'Is this what users want and expect?' Sometimes it really is that simple.
key_insight: Complaints about Apple's new per-contact sharing controls are just the predictable resentment of those who were profiting from an exploitative practice that never should have been permitted in the first place.
